bins.name: LLFC flexible interconnection framework
bins.desc: Critical Loop strongly supports creating a tariff-backed Large-Load Flexibility Commitment that credits curtailment-ready loads and co-located storage. The filer argues existing technology makes large loads technically flexible today, and that pairing enforceable limits with time-varying operating envelopes and real-time telemetry unlocks faster connections. Capacity market recognition and BESS bridge operation are companion elements.
quotes: Establish a Large‑Load Flexibility Commitment (LLFC) in the pro forma tariff; an opt‑in construct under which a load accepts specified curtailment and/or relies on on‑site or adjacent supply under defined conditions and require transmission providers to model LLFC parameters explicitly in interconnection studies

transmission providers should evaluate interconnections against those limits with explicit non‑performance contingencies rather than defaulting to a static, all‑resources‑unavailable maximum‑withdrawal assumption that obscures the value of on‑site batteries

the transmission provider should issue time‑varying operating envelopes that cap a site's net withdrawals (and, if applicable, net injections). The customer's certified control system must enforce the envelope autonomously and provide real‑time telemetry confirming compliance
RTOs/ISOs should recognize LLFCs in capacity accreditation or allow appropriate adjustments to procurement obligations when loads accept enforceable limits and provide verifiable response

Recognize BESS‑enabled bridge operation as a low‑impact path that can permit faster than new combustion generation, improves power quality and ride‑through for large loads, and buys time while export or transmission upgrades for new generation proceed
Speed‑to‑power is a core reliability metric for U.S. industry and AI infrastructure; policy should meet the moment

bins.name: Replace static worst-case study assumption
bins.desc: Critical Loop opposes the current default of modeling large loads at static maximum withdrawal with all resources unavailable. It argues this assumption overstates upgrade costs and obscures BESS value. The preferred alternative uses LLFC operating envelopes and explicit non-performance contingencies as the base case, paired with hosting-capacity-style data and a 60-day initial determination to speed planning.
quotes: the bottleneck is the commercial and procedural pathway
these same frameworks can reduce the proliferation of 'phantom' large‑load projects, speculative or oversized requests that sit in queues and distort transmission planning and investment signals

transmission providers should evaluate interconnections against those limits with explicit non‑performance contingencies rather than defaulting to a static, all‑resources‑unavailable maximum‑withdrawal assumption that obscures the value of on‑site batteries

Systematize and automate flexible interconnections by requiring publication of hosting‑capacity‑style data for load (hourly and seasonal headroom, constraints, expected curtailment windows) and by offering standardized flexible options (limited‑load profiles, non‑firm service, and joint‑study paths for hybrid or adjacent resources)

A 60‑day requirement for all large‑load requests should provide a binding initial determination that includes a limited‑load capacity profile, identified constraints and telemetry, and a provisional queue position so developers can plan and finance while full engineering studies continue

When developers must anchor a queue position in transparent hosting‑capacity data, tariff‑backed LLFC commitments, and enforceable telemetry and non‑performance provisions, it becomes far less attractive to hold multiple oversized, purely optional positions that inflate apparent load growth
base upgrade requirements should not be set by those sensitivities when enforceable LLFC controls materially bound risk

bins.name: Upgrade costs tied to actual risk
bins.desc: The filer argues that when enforceable LLFC controls materially bound a site's withdrawal risk, base upgrade requirements should reflect the LLFC envelope rather than the non-performance sensitivity. This would lower interconnection costs for flexible loads.
quotes: base upgrade requirements should not be set by those sensitivities when enforceable LLFC controls materially bound risk

bins.name: Co-located and adjacent supply credit
bins.desc: Critical Loop asks FERC to treat co-located or adjacent resources behind the same point of interconnection as creditable under the LLFC, with joint study of load and supply. The framework should be resource-agnostic so long as a certified power-control system enforces import limits and non-export at the POI.
quotes: Create a non‑firm network service (withdrawal) option and allow non‑firm injection for hybrid or adjacent resources participating in LLFCs, with joint study for load and supply at or near a common point of interconnection

bins.name: Hybrid non-firm service rights
bins.desc: Critical Loop asks for non-firm withdrawal rights for LLFC participants and non-firm injection rights for co-located or adjacent supply, with joint study at or near a common point of interconnection.
quotes: Establish a Large‑Load Flexibility Commitment (LLFC) in the pro forma tariff; an opt‑in construct under which a load accepts specified curtailment and/or relies on on‑site or adjacent supply under defined conditions and require transmission providers to model LLFC parameters explicitly in interconnection studies

Create a non‑firm network service (withdrawal) option and allow non‑firm injection for hybrid or adjacent resources participating in LLFCs, with joint study for load and supply at or near a common point of interconnection

bins.name: Joint study for load and supply
bins.desc: The filer recommends that hosting-capacity-style data publication and standardized joint-study paths cover both load and hybrid or adjacent supply at the same point of interconnection, reducing soft costs and directing requests to viable locations.
quotes: Systematize and automate flexible interconnections by requiring publication of hosting‑capacity‑style data for load (hourly and seasonal headroom, constraints, expected curtailment windows) and by offering standardized flexible options (limited‑load profiles, non‑firm service, and joint‑study paths for hybrid or adjacent resources)

Create a non‑firm network service (withdrawal) option and allow non‑firm injection for hybrid or adjacent resources participating in LLFCs, with joint study for load and supply at or near a common point of interconnection

bins.name: Expedited path for flexible loads
bins.desc: Critical Loop asks for a 60-day binding initial determination for all large-load requests, giving developers a provisional queue position while full studies proceed. It also supports recognizing BESS bridge operation as a fast-permitting, low-impact path to accelerate time-to-power.
quotes: A 60‑day requirement for all large‑load requests should provide a binding initial determination that includes a limited‑load capacity profile, identified constraints and telemetry, and a provisional queue position so developers can plan and finance while full engineering studies continue

Recognize BESS‑enabled bridge operation as a low‑impact path that can permit faster than new combustion generation, improves power quality and ride‑through for large loads, and buys time while export or transmission upgrades for new generation proceed

bins.name: LLFC as pro forma tariff construct
bins.desc: The central proposal is a tariff-backed opt-in commitment with specified curtailment triggers, maximum hours or MWh per year, notice windows, telemetry, and financial consequences for non-performance, modeled explicitly in interconnection studies.
quotes: Establish a Large‑Load Flexibility Commitment (LLFC) in the pro forma tariff; an opt‑in construct under which a load accepts specified curtailment and/or relies on on‑site or adjacent supply under defined conditions and require transmission providers to model LLFC parameters explicitly in interconnection studies

bins.name: Static assumption masks battery value
bins.desc: Critical Loop argues the standard worst-case study treats co-located BESS as absent or charging and the load as non-curtailable, producing overstated upgrades and delayed connections that do not reflect how an LLFC site actually operates.
quotes: transmission providers should evaluate interconnections against those limits with explicit non‑performance contingencies rather than defaulting to a static, all‑resources‑unavailable maximum‑withdrawal assumption that obscures the value of on‑site batteries

bins.name: Time-varying operating envelopes
bins.desc: The filer proposes that transmission providers issue time-varying operating envelopes capping a site's net withdrawals. A certified customer control system would enforce the envelope autonomously with real-time SCADA-grade telemetry.
quotes: the transmission provider should issue time‑varying operating envelopes that cap a site's net withdrawals (and, if applicable, net injections). The customer's certified control system must enforce the envelope autonomously and provide real‑time telemetry confirming compliance

bins.name: Hosting-capacity data for load
bins.desc: Critical Loop asks FERC to require publication of hourly and seasonal headroom, expected curtailment windows, and binding constraints for load at candidate interconnection points, paralleling distribution-level hosting-capacity or ICA tools.
quotes: Systematize and automate flexible interconnections by requiring publication of hosting‑capacity‑style data for load (hourly and seasonal headroom, constraints, expected curtailment windows) and by offering standardized flexible options (limited‑load profiles, non‑firm service, and joint‑study paths for hybrid or adjacent resources)

bins.name: Reducing speculative queue entries
bins.desc: The filer links tariff-backed LLFC commitments and enforceable telemetry requirements to deterring speculative or oversized queue positions. When a queue position must be anchored in real data and binding commitments, holding multiple optional positions becomes unattractive.
quotes: these same frameworks can reduce the proliferation of 'phantom' large‑load projects, speculative or oversized requests that sit in queues and distort transmission planning and investment signals

When developers must anchor a queue position in transparent hosting‑capacity data, tariff‑backed LLFC commitments, and enforceable telemetry and non‑performance provisions, it becomes far less attractive to hold multiple oversized, purely optional positions that inflate apparent load growth

bins.name: Capacity credit for LLFC loads
bins.desc: Critical Loop urges RTOs and ISOs to recognize LLFC-committed loads in capacity accreditation or reduce procurement obligations accordingly, with performance testing and penalties aligned to peak grid stress periods.
quotes: RTOs/ISOs should recognize LLFCs in capacity accreditation or allow appropriate adjustments to procurement obligations when loads accept enforceable limits and provide verifiable response

bins.name: 60-day initial determination
bins.desc: The filer asks for a binding 60-day initial determination that provides a limited-load capacity profile, identified constraints, telemetry requirements, and a provisional queue position so developers can plan and finance while engineering studies continue.
quotes: A 60‑day requirement for all large‑load requests should provide a binding initial determination that includes a limited‑load capacity profile, identified constraints and telemetry, and a provisional queue position so developers can plan and finance while full engineering studies continue

bins.name: BESS bridge as fast-permit path
bins.desc: Critical Loop asks FERC to recognize BESS-enabled bridge operation as a low-impact, fast-permitting alternative that improves power quality and ride-through while new generation or transmission upgrades proceed.
quotes: Recognize BESS‑enabled bridge operation as a low‑impact path that can permit faster than new combustion generation, improves power quality and ride‑through for large loads, and buys time while export or transmission upgrades for new generation proceed

bins.name: Commercial and procedural bottleneck
bins.desc: The filer notes that the technology to operate large loads flexibly exists today and that the barrier is the commercial and procedural pathway, not the technology.
quotes: the bottleneck is the commercial and procedural pathway

bins.name: Speed-to-power as reliability metric
bins.desc: Critical Loop frames rapid interconnection as a core reliability metric for U.S. industry and AI infrastructure, calling on FERC policy to match that urgency.
quotes: Speed‑to‑power is a core reliability metric for U.S. industry and AI infrastructure; policy should meet the moment
